model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets the model process far more unique training examples within the same verifier horizon. For a small CPU MLP, this is a simple, high-upside way to improve early-training validation loss without adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The successful step strongly suggests the fixed verifier is data-limited rather than update-limited. Pushing batch size from 256 to 512 should expose the model to roughly twice as many examples within the same 256-step budget, while remaining simple and compatible with the current optimization setup.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Both successful edits came from increasing the number of examples consumed under the fixed 256-step budget. Raising batch size again to 1024 is the simplest continuation of that signal and should let this small MLP cover substantially more of CIFAR-10 within the same verifier horizon.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last three accepted steps all improved validation loss by increasing sample throughput under the fixed 256-step budget. Moving from 1024 to 2048 continues that same high-signal direction and should let this small MLP cover essentially the full training set many times within the verifier horizon.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch size has increased by a large factor while the learning rate stayed fixed. At 2048 examples per step, the current setup is likely under-aggressive, so doubling the Adam learning rate is a simple way to better match the larger-batch regime without adding architectural complexity or more wall time.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization setup now trains over many effective passes of the dataset, so the next likely bottleneck is model capacity rather than exposure. Increasing the base width from 192 to 256 hidden units is a compact architecture change that should give the MLP more representational headroom while preserving the same training loop and budget usage.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After increasing batch throughput and modestly widening the network, the next clean capacity increase is one more hidden layer. A deeper MLP should improve representational power for CIFAR-10 while preserving the now-strong optimization setup and keeping the edit minimal.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent wins continue to come from adding capacity on top of the strong large-batch training regime. Increasing base width again, from 256 to 320 hidden units before expansion, is the next minimal architecture step with a reasonable chance of further lowering loss without changing the training logic.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The recent capacity increase overshot with the current Adam setup, which suggests optimization and regularization are now more important than adding more width. Switching from Adam to AdamW is a minimal optimizer-family change that often behaves better in large-batch, weight-decayed regimes like this one.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The last two experiments suggest the best region is the earlier depth-3, width-16 Adam configuration, but that setup may still be overfitting under the large-batch multi-epoch regime. Reverting to the stronger backbone and adding a small amount of dropout is a compact regularization test with a realistic chance to beat the current best.","secondary_modes":["layout","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The dropout test hurt, so the stronger region still appears to be the depth-3, width-16 Adam model without extra regularization. From there, the remaining likely bottleneck is the schedule itself: with only 256 optimizer steps, cosine decay may be annealing too aggressively, so keeping the larger learning rate constant is a compact way to test whether late-training under-updating is limiting validation loss.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Disabling cosine decay improved loss, which indicates the model benefits from staying in a higher-learning-rate regime throughout the short 256-step budget. A modest increase from 2e-3 to 3e-3 is the cleanest next test to see whether the current configuration is still under-stepping rather than capacity-limited.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The higher constant learning rate overshot, so the best region still appears to be the 2e-3 no-schedule setup. From there, the model looks more underfit than overfit: extra width and depth helped, while dropout hurt, so slightly reducing weight decay is a focused regularization adjustment with a realistic chance to improve validation loss.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Reducing weight decay from 1e-4 to 5e-5 improved the best loss, which suggests the current large-batch, no-schedule regime is still slightly over-regularized. A smaller step down to 2e-5 continues that trend conservatively without changing the optimizer, architecture, or wall-time profile.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 100.0)","old":"            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 5.0)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The current configuration is stable and improving only incrementally, so the next useful test is to remove a possible optimization bottleneck without changing capacity or wall time. With large batches, Adam, and no schedule, clipping at 5.0 may be unnecessarily constraining updates; relaxing it sharply preserves safety while allowing larger effective steps if clipping is currently active.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest improvements in this run came from increasing data throughput under the fixed 256-step budget, and the current configuration appears stable enough to support another jump. Moving to batch size 4096 is a high-upside late-run probe that could further reduce loss by turning the fixed-step budget into even more effective dataset coverage without changing the learned configuration otherwise.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The latest large gain came from doubling batch size again, while the learning rate stayed fixed. Since 3e-3 was too aggressive at batch size 2048 but the regime has now changed materially, retesting 3e-3 at 4096 is a high-upside way to better match step size to the much larger batch without adding complexity elsewhere.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At batch size 4096, raising the learning rate to 3e-3 clearly overshot, so the strongest region remains the 2e-3 large-batch setup. Since reducing weight decay consistently helped as training became more data-rich, the next high-signal test is to pair the stronger 2e-3 setting with zero weight decay and see whether the model still benefits from less regularization at this scale.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 8192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant pattern across this run is that, under the fixed 256-step verifier budget, larger batches repeatedly unlocked major loss reductions. With only one step remaining and the current configuration stable, the highest-upside final probe is to double batch size again and see whether even more example throughput can push validation loss lower.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}
